one_liner: A 52-year-old man presents with 3 months of chronic sinusitis, persistent epistaxis, and new hemoptysis, and on review is noted to have dark cola-colored urine for the past week.

stage2.hpi_extension: For 3 months he has had **persistent sinus pain, purulent nasal drainage, and recurrent epistaxis** that has not responded to multiple courses of antibiotics and decongestants. Over the past month he has developed a **chronic cough productive of blood-tinged sputum** and two episodes of **frank hemoptysis**. He reports 6 kg of unintentional weight loss, low-grade fevers, fatigue, and migratory joint pains. In the last week his urine has become **dark and cola-colored** and his ankles are mildly swollen. He also notes a persistent 'stuffy' right ear.
stage2.pmh: 'Recurrent sinus infections' for the past year
stage2.social_history: High-school teacher. Non-smoker. No occupational dust exposure.
stage2.physical_exam: HEENT: **crusting and ulceration of the nasal mucosa**, early **'saddle-nose' deformity** with loss of nasal bridge support, sinus tenderness, serous right otitis media. Lungs: scattered bilateral crackles. Cardiac: regular, no rubs. Abdomen: soft. Extremities: mild bilateral ankle edema, palpable purpura on both lower legs. Neuro: decreased sensation and weakness of the right foot consistent with a foot drop (**mononeuritis multiplex**).

lab_results.result: **c-ANCA (cytoplasmic pattern) positive**. **Anti-proteinase 3 (anti-PR3) antibody positive** at high titer. p-ANCA/anti-MPO negative. ANA negative. Anti-GBM negative. Complement levels normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: c-ANCA/anti-PR3 is highly sensitive and specific for **granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener)** and fits the classic upper airway + lower airway + renal triad.

lab_results.result: 3+ blood, 2+ protein, **dysmorphic red blood cells and red blood cell casts** on microscopy. Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (rising from a baseline of 1.0).
lab_results.clinical_significance: RBC casts and dysmorphic RBCs indicate **glomerulonephritis**; the rapidly rising creatinine is consistent with **rapidly progressive (crescentic) glomerulonephritis (RPGN)**.

lab_results.result: Chest CT: **multiple bilateral nodular opacities with cavitation** in both lungs. Sinus CT: mucosal thickening and bony erosion of the nasal septum.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Cavitary pulmonary nodules in combination with destructive sinus disease and RPGN constitute the **upper airway, lower airway, and renal triad** of granulomatosis with polyangiitis.

stage5.final_clue: Kidney biopsy shows **pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis**. He is started on **induction therapy with IV methylprednisolone pulses plus cyclophosphamide** (rituximab is an alternative), with rapid improvement in renal function and pulmonary infiltrates. Maintenance therapy with azathioprine is planned after induction.

debrief.classic_presentation: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA; formerly Wegener granulomatosis) is an ANCA-associated small-to-medium vessel vasculitis that classically presents with the **triad of upper airway + lower airway + renal** involvement. Upper airway: chronic sinusitis, nasal crusting and ulceration, **saddle-nose deformity**, otitis. Lower airway: cough, **hemoptysis**, **cavitary pulmonary nodules**. Renal: **rapidly progressive (crescentic) glomerulonephritis** with hematuria, RBC casts, and rising creatinine. Constitutional symptoms (fever, weight loss), arthralgias, skin purpura, and **mononeuritis multiplex** are common.
debrief.pathophysiology: GPA is a **necrotizing granulomatous vasculitis** of small and medium vessels, mediated by **anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies** directed against **proteinase 3 (c-ANCA/anti-PR3)**. Histopathology shows **necrotizing granulomatous inflammation of the respiratory tract** and **pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis** in the kidney (few or no immune deposits on immunofluorescence). This distinguishes GPA from immune-complex glomerulonephritides such as lupus nephritis and IgA nephropathy.
debrief.treatment_overview: Induction therapy for severe/organ-threatening disease uses **corticosteroids (IV methylprednisolone pulses then oral prednisone) plus cyclophosphamide or rituximab**. Plasmapheresis may be added for severe alveolar hemorrhage or rapidly progressive GN. After remission, **maintenance therapy** transitions to **azathioprine, methotrexate, or rituximab** to minimize cyclophosphamide toxicity. **Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis** is important while on heavy immunosuppression to prevent Pneumocystis pneumonia.
high_yield_pearls: Triad = Upper airway (sinusitis, saddle-nose) + Lower airway (cavitary nodules, hemoptysis) + Renal (RPGN with RBC casts).
c-ANCA / anti-PR3 positive is highly specific for GPA; p-ANCA / anti-MPO is more often seen in microscopic polyangiitis and EGPA.
Renal biopsy: pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis (no/few immune deposits)  -  distinguishes ANCA vasculitis from immune-complex disease.
Lung biopsy / tissue: necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with giant cells  -  contrast with the non-granulomatous necrotizing vasculitis of microscopic polyangiitis.
Induction: steroids + cyclophosphamide or rituximab; maintenance: azathioprine, methotrexate, or rituximab; add TMP-SMX for PCP prophylaxis.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017)  -  Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Vasculitides  -  Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener)' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017)  -  Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'ANCA associations in small-vessel vasculitis'.
